questions.question: For a short, parallelizable CPU-bound unit of work that you want to await and observe for exceptions, which is the recommended default?
options: `Task.Run`, which queues to the thread pool and returns an observable `Task`
`new Thread(...)`, to get a dedicated OS thread for the work
A named `Mutex` to coordinate the worker
`Parallel.Invoke`, since it always uses the calling thread
questions.explanation: `Task.Run` is the modern default for short CPU-bound work: it uses the pool's efficiency and hands back a `Task` you can await and observe for exceptions. `new Thread` is reserved for long-lived dedicated work; a `Mutex` is a synchronization primitive, not a way to run work.

questions.question: Which statement about foreground vs background threads in .NET is correct?
options: A `new Thread` defaults to foreground and will keep the process alive unless `IsBackground = true` is set
Thread-pool threads are foreground and keep the process alive until all queued work completes
`Task.Run` work runs on a foreground thread, so it always finishes before the process exits
Setting `IsBackground = true` makes a thread keep the process alive longer
questions.explanation: `new Thread` defaults to foreground, and a process stays alive while any foreground thread runs; you must set `IsBackground = true` to opt out. Thread-pool threads and `Task.Run` work are background and do not keep the process alive.

questions.question: What is the correct way to stop in-progress work on a thread in modern .NET?
options: Cooperative cancellation via a `CancellationToken`
`Thread.Abort()`, which raises an exception on the target thread
`Thread.Interrupt()` followed by `Thread.Abort()`
Setting `Thread.Priority` to the lowest value so the work stops
questions.explanation: `Thread.Abort` is removed/obsolete on modern .NET (it throws `PlatformNotSupportedException` on .NET 5+); cooperative cancellation via `CancellationToken` is the only correct mechanism. Lowering priority only deprioritizes, it does not stop work.

questions.question: What does this code reliably print, and why?
options: Usually less than 100000, varying per run, because `counter++` is a non-atomic read-modify-write executed concurrently
Always exactly 100000, because `Parallel.For` serializes the body internally
Always exactly 100000, because `int` increments are atomic in .NET
Always 0, because each parallel partition gets its own copy of `counter`
questions.explanation: `counter++` is read-add-write; with many threads running concurrently, increments are lost to the race, so the result is usually below 100000 and varies. The fix is `Interlocked.Increment` or a thread-local accumulator combined at the end.

questions.question: Which statements about `System.Threading.Tasks.Parallel` (`For`/`ForEach`/`Invoke`) are correct? Select all that apply.
options: It is synchronous from the caller's perspective — it blocks the calling thread until all partitions finish
`MaxDegreeOfParallelism` via `ParallelOptions` bounds how many iterations run at once
Exceptions thrown by the body are aggregated into an `AggregateException`
It is the recommended tool for I/O-bound iterations that each do an async download
Early exit is done with a plain `break`/`return` inside the body
questions.explanation: `Parallel.*` is synchronous data parallelism that blocks the caller, honors `MaxDegreeOfParallelism`, and aggregates body exceptions into an `AggregateException`. It is for CPU-bound work, not I/O (use `Parallel.ForEachAsync`/async), and early exit uses `ParallelLoopState` (`Stop()`/`Break()`), not `break`/`return`.

questions.question: Using the reviewer's four-part test, which situation is a race condition?
options: Shared mutable state touched by two or more threads where at least one writes, with no synchronization
Multiple threads reading the same immutable object concurrently
Two threads each writing to their own thread-local field that is never shared
A single thread performing a non-atomic read-modify-write in a loop
questions.explanation: A race requires shared, mutable state, touched by 2+ threads, with at least one writer, and no synchronization. Reading immutable data, writing only thread-local fields, or single-threaded code each fail one of those conditions, so none is a race.

questions.question: On a 32-bit platform, reads and writes of a `long` field are not guaranteed atomic and can be observed half-updated (torn), so `Interlocked.Read` or a lock is needed to read it safely.
questions.explanation: Values larger than a native word (e.g. `long`/`double` on 32-bit) are not guaranteed atomic and can tear; `Interlocked.Read` gives an atomic 64-bit read on 32-bit platforms, or a lock can be used.

questions.question: Which object is the correct choice to pass to a `lock` statement?
options: A private, readonly, reference-type object dedicated to that lock
`this`, so callers can see what is being locked
A string constant, since strings are convenient lock keys
The `Type` object of the class, e.g. `typeof(MyClass)`
questions.explanation: You should lock on a private readonly reference object dedicated to the lock so no external code can take the same monitor. Locking on `this`, a `Type`, or a string (interned, shared process-wide) lets outside code lock the same object and deadlock you.

questions.question: You need single-writer mutual exclusion but must `await` inside the critical section. Why can't you use `lock`, and what should you use instead?
options: The compiler forbids `await` inside `lock` because `Monitor` is thread-affine; use a `SemaphoreSlim(1, 1)` with `WaitAsync`
`lock` works fine with `await`; there is no reason to change it
`lock` is too slow for async code; use a named `Mutex` with `WaitOne`
`await` inside `lock` compiles but silently disables the lock; use `volatile` instead
questions.explanation: You cannot `await` inside a `lock` — the compiler forbids it because the monitor is thread-affine and a continuation may resume on a different thread. The async-safe substitute is `SemaphoreSlim(1, 1)` with `await WaitAsync()` and `Release()` in a `finally`.

questions.question: What does `Interlocked.CompareExchange(ref x, newVal, comparand)` do, and what is its return value?
options: Atomically sets `x = newVal` only if `x == comparand`, and returns the original value of `x`
Atomically sets `x = newVal` only if `x == comparand`, and returns the new value
Atomically sets `x = comparand` only if `x == newVal`, and returns `true`/`false`
Always sets `x = newVal` regardless of `comparand`, and returns the old value
questions.explanation: CAS sets `x = newVal` only when `x` currently equals `comparand`, and always returns the original value of `x`. Comparing that returned original against `comparand` is how a lock-free retry loop detects whether another thread changed the value.

questions.question: In this lock-free atomic-max routine, what causes the `do/while` loop to iterate more than once?
options: Another thread changed `target` between the `Volatile.Read` and the CAS, so the CAS returns a value other than `snapshot`
`value` is larger than `snapshot`, which always forces a second pass
`Volatile.Read` is non-atomic and may return a torn value that fails the comparison
`Interlocked.CompareExchange` always returns `value`, never `snapshot`
questions.explanation: The CAS succeeds only if `target` still equals `snapshot`; if another thread changed `target` in between, CAS returns the new (different) value, the condition is true, and the loop retries with a fresh snapshot. This optimistic retry is the standard lock-free pattern.

questions.question: Which statements about `SemaphoreSlim` are correct? Select all that apply.
options: `SemaphoreSlim(1, 1)` with `WaitAsync` is the standard async substitute for `lock`
It is not reentrant and not thread-affine — a different thread (e.g. an async continuation) can `Release`
`Release()` should be placed in a `finally` block so it always runs
It is reentrant like `Monitor`, so the same logical flow can re-acquire it safely
Only the thread that called `Wait` is permitted to call `Release`
questions.explanation: `SemaphoreSlim(1,1)` + `WaitAsync` is the async mutex, `Release` belongs in a `finally`, and unlike `Monitor` it is neither reentrant nor thread-affine — any thread may release it. Because it is not reentrant, re-acquiring on the same logical flow deadlocks, and there is no owning-thread restriction on `Release`.

questions.question: When is reaching for `System.Threading.Mutex` over `lock`/`SemaphoreSlim` actually justified?
options: When you need cross-process synchronization, e.g. a single-instance app using a named mutex
When you need the fastest possible in-process mutual exclusion
When you need to `await` inside the critical section without blocking a thread
When you want many concurrent readers but a single writer
questions.explanation: A named `Mutex` is system-wide, so its real advantage is synchronizing across separate processes (e.g. single-instance enforcement). It is slow because of the kernel transition, can't be awaited, and offers no reader/writer distinction, so in-process it is just a slower `lock`.

questions.question: If the thread owning a `Mutex` exits without releasing it, the next waiter receives an `AbandonedMutexException`, which signals that shared state protected by the mutex may be corrupt.
questions.explanation: An abandoned mutex throws `AbandonedMutexException` to the next waiter precisely because the previous owner left without an orderly release, so the protected state may be in an inconsistent/corrupt condition.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `EnterUpgradeableReadLock` on `ReaderWriterLockSlim`, and what is its key restriction?
options: It lets a thread read while reserving the right to upgrade to a write lock without releasing first; only one upgradeable reader is allowed at a time
It lets unlimited threads hold upgradeable read locks so any of them can later become the writer
It is a faster form of `EnterReadLock` with no ability to write
It promotes all current readers to writers simultaneously when contention is detected
questions.explanation: The upgradeable read lock is the safe way to do read-check-then-write: it reads while reserving the right to upgrade to a write lock, but only one upgradeable reader is permitted at a time. The other options misstate either the count restriction or the read/write behavior.

questions.question: What does the `volatile` keyword guarantee for a field?
options: Ordering/visibility (acquire/release semantics), but not atomicity
Atomicity, so `volatile int x; x++;` is thread-safe
Both ordering and atomicity for all field types including `long`
Mutual exclusion, equivalent to wrapping each access in a `lock`
questions.explanation: `volatile` gives a field acquire/release ordering and visibility, but not atomicity, so `x++` (a read-modify-write) is still a race. It is not a lock and provides no mutual exclusion.

questions.question: If access to shared state already goes through a `lock` or a concurrent collection, you also need to mark the underlying field `volatile` to guarantee visibility.
questions.explanation: `lock`, `Interlocked`, and `Task`/`await` boundaries already establish the necessary memory barriers, so adding `volatile` on top is redundant — the reviewer explicitly says you do not also need `volatile` in that case.

questions.question: `ConcurrentDictionary.GetOrAdd` is thread-safe, so why can its value-factory delegate run more than once, and how do you guarantee the expensive work happens only once?
options: The factory isn't part of the atomic section, so under contention multiple threads may invoke it (only one result is stored); wrap the value in `Lazy<T>` so the body runs once
The factory runs twice only when the key already exists; call `TryGetValue` first to avoid it
It is a bug in `ConcurrentDictionary`; switch to a plain `Dictionary` guarded by a `lock`
The factory runs once per bucket; reduce the bucket count to force a single invocation
questions.explanation: Under contention several threads can invoke the `GetOrAdd` factory, and only one returned value 'wins' and is stored. Storing a `Lazy<T>` value ensures the expensive factory body executes exactly once even if the outer delegate runs multiple times.

questions.question: Spot the concurrency bug in this hit-counter and the correct fix.
options: It is a check-then-act race across separate atomic calls; replace it with `_hits.AddOrUpdate(key, 1, (_, c) => c + 1)`
`ConcurrentDictionary` indexers aren't thread-safe; wrap the whole method in a `lock`
`ContainsKey` throws under contention; catch the exception and retry
There is no bug, because every `ConcurrentDictionary` operation here is atomic
questions.explanation: Each individual call is atomic, but the sequence of contains/read/write has gaps where another thread can interleave, so updates are lost. The atomic `AddOrUpdate` collapses the read-modify-write into one safe operation.

questions.question: For which workload is `ConcurrentBag<T>` the right concurrent collection, and what should you prefer otherwise?
options: When the same thread both adds and removes (thread-local pools); prefer `ConcurrentQueue<T>` when producers and consumers are different threads
When you need strict FIFO ordering across many producers; otherwise use `ConcurrentStack<T>`
When you need a bounded blocking queue with backpressure; otherwise use `BlockingCollection<T>`
When you need atomic keyed lookups; otherwise use `ConcurrentDictionary<K,V>`
questions.explanation: `ConcurrentBag` uses thread-local storage and is optimized for the producer==consumer case where the same thread adds and removes. When producers and consumers are distinct threads, `ConcurrentQueue` is the better choice; `ConcurrentBag` is unordered, so it is not for FIFO.

questions.question: In a `BlockingCollection<T>` producer/consumer pipeline, how do consumers cleanly drain remaining items and then stop?
options: The producer calls `CompleteAdding()`; consumers iterating `GetConsumingEnumerable()` drain the rest and the `foreach` ends
The producer calls `Dispose()`, which immediately aborts any blocked consumers mid-item
Consumers poll `Count == 0` in a busy loop and break when it stays zero
The producer adds a `null` sentinel and each consumer checks for it
questions.explanation: `CompleteAdding()` signals no more items; consumers iterating `GetConsumingEnumerable()` then drain the remaining items and the `foreach` ends cleanly. That handshake avoids busy-waiting and ad-hoc sentinels.

questions.question: Which techniques help prevent deadlock between two locks? Select all that apply.
options: Impose a consistent global lock-ordering (always acquire A before B)
Use timeouts such as `Monitor.TryEnter` or `WaitAsync(timeout)`
Never call out to unknown code while holding a lock
Always acquire both locks but in whichever order is convenient at each call site
Block on the second lock indefinitely until it becomes free
questions.explanation: A global lock-ordering breaks the circular-wait condition, timeouts let a thread back off instead of waiting forever, and not calling unknown code while holding a lock avoids surprise re-entrancy/deadlock. Acquiring locks in inconsistent order or blocking indefinitely are exactly what cause deadlocks.

questions.question: A service shows high latency, growing request queues, but low CPU utilization. What is the most likely cause and the real fix?
options: Thread-pool starvation from pool threads blocked on `.Result`/`.Wait()`/sync I/O; fix by making the blocking work truly async
CPU saturation from too much parallelism; fix by lowering `MaxDegreeOfParallelism`
A deadlock from inconsistent lock ordering; fix by removing all locks
False sharing on a hot cache line; fix by padding the request objects
questions.explanation: Low CPU with high latency and growing queues is the classic thread-pool starvation signature: pool threads are blocked, so new work waits for the pool's slow ~1-thread-per-0.5 s injection. The cure is to stop blocking pool threads by making the work async; raising `SetMinThreads` is only a temporary mitigation.

questions.question: Two threads each update their own separate counter variable, yet the parallel code is unexpectedly slow while producing perfectly correct results. What is happening?
options: False sharing — the two distinct variables sit on the same CPU cache line, so each write invalidates the other core's cached line
A race condition that corrupts the counters but only under load
Lock convoy — both threads serialize behind one hot lock
Thread-pool starvation because both counters block their pool threads
questions.explanation: False sharing is a performance-only problem (results stay correct) where unrelated variables on the same 64-byte cache line cause cache-coherency ping-pong between cores. A race would corrupt results, and there is no lock or blocking involved here. The fix is to pad/separate the hot per-thread fields onto distinct cache lines.

questions.question: Why does this deadlock when called on a thread with a single-threaded `SynchronizationContext` (legacy UI / classic ASP.NET)?
options: `.Result` blocks the single thread of the captured context, but `GetAsync`'s continuation needs that same context to resume — circular wait
`Task.Delay` never completes because the timer thread is also blocked by `.Result`
`async` methods always run on a new thread that the caller's context cannot reach
`.Result` throws an `AggregateException` that is silently swallowed, hanging the call
questions.explanation: `.Result` blocks the context's only thread; when the awaited `Task.Delay` completes, the continuation must resume on that same captured context, which is still blocked waiting for the result — a circular wait and thus a deadlock. The fix is to `await` all the way up instead of blocking with `.Result`.
